narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Friday, June 5, 2026
Headlines
Daily chances for showers and thunderstorms continue through early next week.
Storms are not expected to be widespread or severe, but a few stronger storms cannot be ruled out.
Heat and humidity will steadily increase through next week.
Sunday and Monday will likely be the coolest days before summer heat settles in by midweek.

The First Warn Forecast begins with a warm and increasingly humid pattern across Missouri and Illinois. A cluster of thunderstorms moving across northern Missouri early this morning will gradually weaken as it pushes eastward. While additional showers and thunderstorms may linger through the morning hours, the overall severe weather threat remains low due to limited instability and weak wind shear.

As we move through the afternoon, skies will become partly sunny with temperatures climbing into the middle and upper 80s. Despite plenty of heat and humidity in place, a cap in the atmosphere should prevent widespread thunderstorm development. There may be an isolated shower or thunderstorm across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, but most areas should remain dry through much of the day.

Tonight, another round of weakening thunderstorms may approach from Iowa toward daybreak Saturday. Most of these storms are expected to weaken before reaching our area, though a few isolated stronger storms could produce small hail or gusty winds if they hold together longer than expected.

Saturday will feel much more like summer. Temperatures will climb into the mid and upper 80s with some locations nearing 90 degrees. Combined with increasing humidity, heat index values will rise into the lower and middle 90s during the afternoon. Scattered showers and thunderstorms are expected to develop, especially during the afternoon and evening hours. However, wind shear will remain weak, which should limit the organization and severity of storms.

The unsettled weather pattern continues Sunday and Monday as an upper-level disturbance slowly moves through the Mid-Mississippi Valley. This system will bring additional opportunities for showers and thunderstorms both days. While severe weather is not anticipated at this time, periods of rain and cloud cover will help keep temperatures somewhat lower compared to the rest of next week.

By Tuesday and especially Wednesday through Friday, a strengthening ridge of high pressure will build across the eastern United States. This will allow temperatures to surge into the 90s while humidity levels continue to rise. Dew points may climb well into the 70s, creating increasingly oppressive conditions. Heat index values will likely exceed the mid-90s, and portions of the region could approach or exceed 100 degrees by late week.

The bottom line is that summer is making a strong return. Daily storm chances will remain in the forecast through early next week, but the bigger story will become the building heat and humidity as we head toward the middle and latter part of next week.

First Warn Weather: First. Accurate. Trusted.
